title: Meckel diverticulum: true diverticulum (all 3 layers)
vignette: A 30-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to severe abdominal pain. The patient first developed the pain yesterday, and it has become increasingly worse. Over the last few hours, he has also developed bilious emesis. The patient has had no previous surgeries. Temperature is 37.8 C (100 F) and pulse is 110/min. Physical examination shows diffuse abdominal tenderness with guarding during palpation. Abdominal imaging reveals bowel wall thickening within a blind pouch connected to the ileum. A laparotomy is performed. During the procedure, a fibrous band is seen attaching the end of the pouch to the umbilicus. The walls of this pouch are most likely composed of which of the following?

clues.note: Acute, worsening abdominal pain with guarding signals an acute surgical abdomen - here from an inflamed/obstructing diverticulum (Meckel diverticulitis).
Bilious vomiting indicates bowel obstruction distal to the ampulla of Vater - a recognized complication of a Meckel diverticulum (via volvulus, intussusception, or inflammation).

clues.text: diffuse abdominal tenderness with guarding during palpation
clues.note: Peritoneal signs (guarding) reflect inflammation - acute diverticulitis of the Meckel pouch causing the surgical presentation.

clues.text: bowel wall thickening within a blind pouch connected to the ileum
clues.note: A blind outpouching off the ILEUM is the location of a Meckel diverticulum (classically ~2 feet from the ileocecal valve), and wall thickening indicates inflammation.

clues.text: fibrous band is seen attaching the end of the pouch to the umbilicus
clues.note: A fibrous band tethering the pouch to the UMBILICUS is the remnant of the vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct - pathognomonic for a Meckel diverticulum. Being a vitelline duct remnant, it is a TRUE diverticulum containing all bowel-wall layers.

whatMatters: A blind ileal pouch with a fibrous band to the umbilicus = Meckel diverticulum, a remnant of the vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct. It is a TRUE diverticulum, so its wall contains ALL THREE layers - mucosa, submucosa, and muscularis (muscular layer). Answer E. False diverticula (e.g., Zenker, colonic) contain only mucosa + submucosa.
strategy: Identify Meckel from the ileal blind pouch + umbilical fibrous band (vitelline duct remnant), then recall true vs false diverticulum. TRUE diverticulum = outpouching of ALL wall layers (mucosa + submucosa + muscularis); congenital, like Meckel. FALSE (pseudo)diverticulum = mucosa + submucosa herniating through a muscular defect (Zenker, colonic). The umbilical attachment guarantees 'true' -> all three layers.

eliminations.label: Fibrous scar tissue - postoperative/inflammatory adhesion
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: scar tissue forms after surgery or chronic inflammation and can cause obstruction. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this patient has had no prior surgery, and a congenital blind pouch tethered to the umbilicus is bowel wall, not scar. ✅ Correct if: adhesive small-bowel obstruction after prior abdominal surgery.

eliminations.label: Granulation tissue and peritoneum - not a diverticulum wall
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: granulation tissue is new vascular connective tissue of wound healing; peritoneum lines the abdominal cavity. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: neither composes a Meckel diverticulum, which is true intestinal wall. ✅ Correct if: describing a healing wound bed, not a congenital diverticulum.

eliminations.label: Mucosa and submucosa only - FALSE diverticulum (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a pseudodiverticulum, where mucosa + submucosa herniate through a defect in the muscular layer (e.g., Zenker, colonic diverticulosis). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Meckel is a TRUE diverticulum (vitelline duct remnant) containing all three layers including muscularis - the umbilical band gives away its congenital, full-thickness nature. The trap is defaulting to 'diverticulum = mucosa + submucosa.' ✅ Correct if: acquired Zenker or colonic (false) diverticulum.

eliminations.label: Omentum and adipose tissue - wrong tissue
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: fatty omentum drapes the abdominal organs. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: an omental/adipose mass is not an ileal outpouching with all bowel-wall layers; it doesn't explain a blind pouch on the ileum. ✅ Correct if: an omental tumor/lipoma, not a diverticulum.

reasoning: Acute abdomen + bilious emesis + ileal blind pouch + fibrous band to the umbilicus = Meckel diverticulum
Meckel is a remnant of the vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct - a congenital outpouching
Congenital outpouchings are TRUE diverticula: they contain all bowel-wall layers
-> wall = mucosa + submucosa + muscularis; answer E
mechanism: A Meckel diverticulum results from incomplete obliteration of the vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct, which normally connects the yolk sac to the primitive midgut in early gestation; persistence leaves a blind ileal pouch, sometimes tethered to the umbilicus by a fibrous band as seen here. Because it is a congenital outpouching of the bowel itself, it is a TRUE diverticulum, with a wall containing all three intestinal layers - mucosa, submucosa, and muscularis. It frequently harbors heterotopic gastric (or pancreatic) mucosa that secretes acid and can ulcer adjacent ileum, classically causing painless lower-GI bleeding; other complications include diverticulitis, obstruction, intussusception, and volvulus, producing the acute abdomen and bilious emesis here. False (pseudo)diverticula, by contrast, are acquired herniations of only mucosa and submucosa through a muscular wall defect (e.g., Zenker, colonic diverticulosis).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Embryo/GI > Meckel diverticulum: persistent vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct; TRUE diverticulum (all 3 layers); heterotopic gastric mucosa -> painless hematochezia; 'rule of 2s'; vs false diverticulum (mucosa+submucosa, Zenker/colonic).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: recognize Meckel (ileal pouch + umbilical fibrous band), then apply true (all layers) vs false (mucosa+submucosa) diverticulum.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on true vs false (pseudo) diverticulum, vitelline/omphalomesenteric duct, muscularis, heterotopic mucosa, or bilious emesis? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: 'Mucosa and submucosa' (your pick) is the definition of a FALSE diverticulum and is the classic trap - but Meckel is congenital/TRUE, so it includes the muscular layer too.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The true-vs-false diverticulum diagram contrasts a full-thickness (true) pouch with a mucosa/submucosa herniation (false). If it confused you, anchor on 'congenital + umbilical band = true = all 3 layers.'

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: Meckel = vitelline duct remnant, TRUE diverticulum (all 3 layers), heterotopic gastric mucosa -> painless bleeding; Zenker/colonic = FALSE (mucosa + submucosa only).

keywords.front: Blind ileal pouch with fibrous band to the umbilicus

keywords.back: Outpouching of ALL wall layers (mucosa + submucosa + muscularis); e.g., Meckel
Mucosa + submucosa herniate through muscular defect; e.g., Zenker, colonic
Heterotopic gastric mucosa secretes acid -> ulceration -> painless hematochezia
2% of population, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of mucosa, presents by age 2
Embryonic connection of yolk sac to midgut; failure to obliterate -> Meckel

firstAid.detail: Embryo/GI > vitelline duct remnant; TRUE diverticulum (all 3 layers); heterotopic gastric mucosa; rule of 2s.
firstAid.location: Embryo/GI > vitelline duct remnant; TRUE diverticulum (all 3 layers); heterotopic gastric mucosa; rule of 2s.

firstAid.detail: GI > true = all layers (congenital); false = mucosa + submucosa (Zenker, colonic).
firstAid.location: GI > true = all layers (congenital); false = mucosa + submucosa (Zenker, colonic).

firstAid.detail: Embryo > Meckel diverticulum, omphalomesenteric fistula, vitelline cyst/fibrous band.
firstAid.location: Embryo > Meckel diverticulum, omphalomesenteric fistula, vitelline cyst/fibrous band.

memoryHook: Meckel is the 'real deal' diverticulum - congenital, full-thickness, ALL three layers. Only acquired pseudo-diverticula skimp at mucosa + submucosa.
